coredata.dc:title: Critical roles of H2O and O2 in H2O2 photoproduction over biomass derived metal-free catalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yan
Wang, Jiaxuan
Wu, Jie
Zhao, Yajie
Huang, Hui
Liu, Yang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, in-situ transient photoinduced-voltage combined with the continuous wavelet transform was used for the first time to study the critical roles of H2O and O2 in the H2O2 photoproduction. A metal-free photocatalyst containing carbon dots was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using hesperidin as raw material. The photocatalytic H2O2 yield of the catalyst can reach 4480 μmol·L−1 under visible light without sacrificial agent. In addition to being raw materials, we have proved that H2O and O2 can regulate the photogenerated electron transport kinetics, namely, O2 can decelerate low-frequency electron transport, while H2O can accelerate the low-frequency electron transport. This work provides an in-depth understanding on photocatalytic production of H2O2 over composite metal-free photocatalysts and new insight for their design.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hydrogen peroxide
Photocatalysis
Transient photoinduced-voltage
Continuous wavelet transform

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Charge transfer kinetics of photocatalyst by TPV
Exploration on the roles of H2O and O2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of HP-2. (b) High-resolution TEM image of HP-2 with size distribution of in-situ formed CDs. (c) XRD patterns of HP-0 and HP-2. (d) C 1s spectra of HP-0 and HP-2.
(a) Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve of HP-2. (b) The band structure diagram of HP-2. (c) EIS Nyquist plots and (d) Photocurrent curves over time of HP-0 and HP-2.

(a) TPV patterns of HP-0 and HP-2. (b) Charge extraction rate (tmax), (c) maximum extraction amount (A), (d) electron decay constant (τ) during charge recombination of HP-0 and HP-2. (e) tmax, (f) τ, (g) A, (h) effective charge extraction amount (ne) (n
e
=A*τ/t
max ) value obtained by TPV patterns of HP-X.

Continuous wavelet transform (CWT) analysis of HP-0 and HP-2. (a) FFT spectrum of HP-2, (b) Two-dimensional (2D) CWT spectrum of HP-2, (c) The delay time difference of the peaks of HP-2 (t′) and HP-0 (t) at different frequencies. (d) Intensity-frequency curves of HP-2 at different time.

(a) The H2O2 production by photocatalysts with different hydrothermal times. (b) The curve of H2O2 production over time. (c) The cyclic stability of HP-2. (The single cycle time is 4 h) (d) The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of HP-2 at different wavelengths in air.

(a) The H2O2 production by HP-2 in different atmospheres. (b) The H2O2 production by HP-2 with different sacrificial agents. (c) DMPO spin-trapping EPR spectra of HP-2 for detecting ·O2 - under dark and light conditions (λ ≥ 420 nm). (d) ORR LSV curves in 0.1 M Na2SO4 for HP-0 and HP-2. HRTEM images of (e) reduction sites and (f) oxidation sites of HP-2. (The red, black, and blue circles represent Ag, CDs and PbO2 particles, respectively.).

(a) The in-situ TPV patterns of HP-0 under different reaction conditions. (b) The in-situ TPV patterns of HP-2 under different reaction conditions. (c) The in-situ TPV curves and integration of HP-2 under N2 and O2-saturated acetonitrile. (d) The in-situ TPV patterns of HP-2 under N2-saturated acetonitrile and 0.5 vol% water/acetonitrile acetonitrile.

Intensity-time curve of in-situ TPV curves for HP-2 at (a) 3 Hz and (b) 4 Hz under different reaction conditions. (c) The delay time difference of the peaks for HP-2 in 0.5 vol% water/acetonitrile and saturated N2 atmosphere. (d) The delay time difference of the peaks for HP-2 in saturated N2 and saturated O2 atmosphere. (e) EIS Nyquist plots of HP-2 in 0.5 vol% water/acetonitrile and saturated N2 atmosphere. (f) EIS Nyquist plots of HP-2 in saturated N2 and saturated O2 atmosphere. (g) The roles of H2O and O2 in photocatalytic process. (ve-H2O, ve-N2 and ve-O2 represent the electron transfer rates in 0.5 vol% water/acetonitrile, saturated N2 and saturated O2, respectively.).

head.ce:title: Critical roles of H2O and O2 in H2O2 photoproduction over biomass derived metal-free catalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, in-situ transient photoinduced-voltage combined with the continuous wavelet transform was used for the first time to study the critical roles of H2O and O2 in the H2O2 photoproduction. A metal-free photocatalyst containing carbon dots was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using hesperidin as raw material. The photocatalytic H2O2 yield of the catalyst can reach 4480 μmol·L−1 under visible light without sacrificial agent. In addition to being raw materials, we have proved that H2O and O2 can regulate the photogenerated electron transport kinetics, namely, O2 can decelerate low-frequency electron transport, while H2O can accelerate the low-frequency electron transport. This work provides an in-depth understanding on photocatalytic production of H2O2 over composite metal-free photocatalysts and new insight for their design.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A metal-free catalyst containing CDs was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method for efficient production of hydrogen peroxide.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), as a green energy carrier and high-energy oxidant that can be safely stored and transported, has been widely used in various industrial fields [1–9]. However, the anthraquinone oxidation method currently used in industrial for H2O2 synthesis suffers from the drawbacks of high energy consumption and heavy pollution [10–15]. Therefore, a sustainable and safe, environmentally friendly, low-cost and efficient method for H2O2 production is urgently needed [16–18]. The direct production of H2O2 from water (H2O) and oxygen (O2) via photocatalysis is considered to be the most ideal method [19–28].

Among the various non-metallic catalysts that have been reported, biomass catalysts have been used in the field of photocatalytic H2O2 production due to their high earth abundance and good biocompatibility [29–32]. However, due to the rapid recombination of photogenerated charges, single-component biomass catalysts are difficult to be used independently for photocatalytic reactions, so sacrificial agents or co-catalysts need to be added in the catalytic process, which will cause environmental harm during the recycling process [33–36]. In addition, most of the currently studied biomass photocatalysts contain elements such as C, N, O and S, which are easily affected by H2O and O2 during the catalytic process [37–40]. So far, H2O and O2 have only been studied as reactants for photocatalysis to generate H2O2, ignoring a deeper understanding of their key roles in photocatalysis [41–47]. These in-depth understanding of reactant-structure-activity relationship in the photocatalysts system is inevitable, where the deeper understanding and interface design should be the key for the photocatalyst design.

In this work, a metal-free catalyst (HP-2) was synthesized from hesperidin by one-step hydrothermal method. The highly efficient production of H2O2 was achieved with the yield of 4480 μmol L−1 without the addition of sacrificing agent. Transient photoinduced-voltage (TPV) test proved that the in-situ generation of carbon dots (CDs) was beneficial to the extraction and separation of photogenerated charges and improved catalytic activity and stability [48,49]. Significantly, this work combines continuous wavelet transform (CWT) and in-situ TPV tests to explore the effects of H2O and O2 on charge transport for the first time. It turned out that O2 can slow down the transfer of low-frequency electrons, while H2O can accelerate the transport of low-frequency electrons. Our work provides new ideas for the low-cost, green, and efficient production of H2O2, as well as new strategies for designing metal-free photocatalysts.

All these materials were used directly without any treatment. Nitrogen and oxygen (98%) were purchased from Adamas. Anhydrous acetonitrile was purchased from J&K. Hesperidin powder was purchased from Bidepharm. H2SO4 (AR, 98%) was purchased from Jiangsu Qiangsheng Functional Chemical Co., Ltd. KMnO4 titration standard solution (0.004 mol/L) was purchased from AKX.

HP-2 was obtained by a one-step hydrothermal method. Hesperidin (100 mg) was dispersed in 30 mL sulfuric acid solution (1.5 mol/L) and stirred for 0.5 h. Then, the mixed solution was placed in Teflon-lined autoclaves and hydrothermally heated at 150 ℃ for 2 h. After the reaction, the solution was centrifuged, washed with ultrapure water to neutral, and freeze-dried to obtain the HP-2 catalyst. The catalysts with different hydrothermal time were named HP-X (X stands for hydrothermal time), where HP-0, HP-0.5, HP-1, HP-2, HP-3 and HP-4 respectively represent the hydrothermal time of hesperidin in sulfuric acid is 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3 and 4 h.

Experiments for the production of H2O2 by photocatalysis were carried out in a quartz bottle containing 10 mg of catalyst, ultrapure water (10 mL) without any sacrificial agent. The suspension was continuously stirred under light (λ ≥ 420 nm) for 4 h in air by a multi-channel photochemical reaction system shown in Fig. S1 (PCX 50B, Beijing perfect-light Co., Ltd, China). Then, the reaction solution was centrifuged, and the obtained solution was filtered through a filter. Finally, the photocatalytic activity was evaluated by measuring the amount of H2O2 generated in the supernatant by titration with potassium permanganate. The photocatalytic performance test under saturated O2 and saturated N2 is to continuously blow high-purity O2 and high-purity N2 into the test solution for 0.5 h, and then carry out the photocatalytic test.

Fig. 1a exhibits transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of HP-2. Combined with the scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of HP-2 in Fig. S3b, the HP-2 displays a porous sheet-like structure. Fig. S3a presents SEM image of the HP-0, which is rod-like structure, indicating that the morphology of hesperidin has been significantly changed after hydrothermal treatment. The enlarged view of Fig. 1a in Fig. 1b manifests CDs are uniformly distributed on the catalyst surface, showing that the hydrothermal process contributes to the generation of CDs. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in the inset of Fig. 1b presents that the lattice spacing of is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of CDs [50,51]. The crystal structure of HP-2 was studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) in Fig. 1c, the black and red curves represent the diffraction peaks of HP-0 and HP-2, respectively. It can be observed that HP-2 is a typical graphitic amorphous carbon structure with an obvious a broad peak around 23°, which is consistent with the diffraction peak of CDs, representing the successful synthesis of CDs. In order to analyze the changes of chemical bond types of the catalysts before and after hydrothermal treatment, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) as shown in Fig. S4 was applied to characterize the catalysts. Compared with HP-0, the carbonization of HP-2 in the hydrothermal process promotes the formation of CDs, resulting in a significant weakening of the C-O bond, and the CO stretching vibration peak appears around 1710 cm−1. The Raman spectra in Fig. S5 clearly displays that HP-2 has two distinct peaks at 1325 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 compared with HP-0, which belong to the D and G peak of CDs [52,53]. The FTIR and Raman spectra of different catalyst samples provided are shown in Fig. S6. When the hydrothermal time is greater than 1 h, the FTIR and Raman spectra of the catalyst hardly change. The C1s spectra of HP-0 and HP-2 in Fig. 1d exhibit CO peak appears at 288.4 eV in the spectrum of HP-2, which is related to the abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs, suggesting the formation of CDs. Fig. S7 shows the ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra of HP-0 and HP-2 in the wavelength range of 300–700 nm. Obviously, the formation of CDs significantly enhances the absorption capacity of the catalyst in the visible light range.

To investigate the band gap structure of the catalyst, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) positions were measured by electrochemical cyclic voltammetry (CV) to ascertain the positions of the conduction band and valence band of HP-2, in which, ferrocene was used as a standard to calibrate the positions of a pair of redox peaks. From the CV curve in Fig. 2a, the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of HP-2 can be calculated as − 6.32 and − 4.11 eV, respectively, and the calculation process is detailed in the Supporting Information. Fig. 2b shows the energy band diagram of HP-2 (E (vs. RHE) = E (vs. EV) + 4.44), indicating that the band structure can simultaneously satisfy the energy band requirements of O2 production from H2O oxidation reaction and H2O2 production from O2 reduction reaction. In addition, the valence band spectrum of HP-2 in Fig. S9 further verifies the accuracy of the band gap. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) as depicted in Fig. 2c investigates the internal resistance (Rct) of charge transfer, HP-2 has a lower internal resistance of charge transfer, indicating that the generation of CDs accelerates the charge transfer. Fig. 2d presents the transient photocurrent response curves of HP-0 and HP-2 at open circuit potential. Obviously, HP-2 has higher photoresponse current, corresponding to higher photogenerated charge separation efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge transfer kinetics of photocatalyst by TPV
ce:section.ce:para: The transient photoinduced-voltage (TPV) experiments were performed to explore the interfacial charge transfer kinetics of the catalysts. As shown in Fig. 3a, the photovoltage of HP-2 under photoexcitation is much higher than that of HP-0, revealing that the generation of CDs facilitates the excitation of photogenerated charge, which is beneficial to catalytic reactions. By analyzing the charge extraction rate (tmax) and the maximum charge extraction efficiency (A) in the TPV amplification curves (Fig. 3b, c), it can be observed that CDs can increases the charge extraction rate of the catalyst (tmax-2＜tmax-1). The A value of HP-2 is much higher than that of HP-0, which suggests that CDs facilitate the amount of photogenerated charge extraction. Furthermore, Fig. 3d displays the τ value of HP-2 is larger than that of HP-0, indicating that the formation of CDs makes the electron decay of HP-2 slower under photoexcitation because the charge storage effect of CDs reduces the recombination of carriers. Besides, PL spectra of HP-0 and HP-2 in Fig. S11 show that HP-0 exhibits strong fluorescence intensity at 506 nm due to severe carrier recombination. On the contrary, the obvious fluorescence quenching observed in HP-2 illustrated that the generation of CDs effectively inhibited the recombination of photogenerated charges and improved the utilization of photogenerated charges.

To investigate the effect of hydrothermal time on the charge transfer, the TPV curves of HP-X under different hydrothermal times were tested, as shown in Fig. S12a. The enlarged views of tmax, τ, A are shown in Fig. S12c-d and the histograms corresponding to the specific values are shown in Fig. 3e-g. It can be observed that tmax shows a decreasing trend with the increase of hydrothermal time, which is due to CDs accelerate the transfer of photogenerated charges and shorten the time required reach maximum charge. Additionally, the τ values of different catalysts show a little difference, but the rate of charge decay slows down after the formation of CDs, which echoes the charge storage properties of CDs. It is exhibited in Fig. 3g that HP-2 has the highest amount of charge extraction, illustrating that the catalyst is able to generate the most photogenerated charge upon photoexcitation. For a more efficient analysis, it is proposed that ne (ne = A*τ/tmax) represents the surface effective number of electrons, Fig. 3h presents the ne values of different catalysts, in theory, HP-2 has the best photocatalytic activity.

To further analyze interfacial electron transfer kinetics, the TPV curves in Fig. 3a of the catalysts were analyzed using fast Fourier transform (FFT) and continuous wavelet transform (CWT). FFT is an alternative representation in which the functional waveform can be decomposed into sine and cosine features. The analysis results of the TPV curves of HP-0 and HP-2 are shown in Fig. 4a. As the frequency increases, the curves gradually decay with no static and periodic frequency components, indicating that the TPV curves have non-static characteristics. The CWT applied in this work is a continuous wavelet analysis method with bior3.9 as the basis function, which is used to analyze the relationship between frequency and time in non-static signals. Figs. 4b and S13a show the two-dimensional (2D) CWT spectra of HP-2 and HP-0, respectively, and the corresponding three-dimensional (3D) CWT spectra are shown in Fig. S13b and c. In addition, the intensity-time curves of HP-0 and HP-2 at different frequencies in Figs. S13 and S14 exhibit there are peaks at lower frequencies in the whole process. However, with the frequency increases, there are CWT peaks within 0–1 ms at 20 Hz, which means that the transmission of high-frequency electrons only occurs in a short time after photo-excitation, while the transmission of low-frequency electrons is present throughout the entire process. In order to better study the behavior of electrons at different frequencies, the time difference between HP-0 and HP-2 reaching peaks at different frequencies is compared in Figs. S13 and S14, the average of the first three peaks was taken when multiple peaks existed. The delay time difference of HP-2 (t′) and HP-0 (t) reaching the peak value at different frequencies is displayed in Fig. 4c. At low frequencies, t′ is greater than t because the CDs formed by the catalyst during the hydrothermal process have the ability to store charges, thereby prolonging the electron lifetime at low frequencies. At high frequencies, t′ is smaller than t, due to the fact that CDs can accelerate charge transport. In conclusion, CDs can not only accelerate the extraction of high-frequency electrons, but also prolong the lifetime of low-frequency electrons. Fig. 4d shows the intensity-frequency relationship curves at different time. At 0.4 ms, the electrons show obvious signals at both low and high frequencies, indicating that both high-frequency and low-frequency electrons play important roles in electron transport within a short time after photoexcitation. When the time increases to 4 ms, the signal of the CWT peak exists only in the low-frequency range, illustrating low-frequency electrons still play a key role in electron transport for a long time after photoexcitation.

The photocatalytic performance of different catalysts was tested under atmospheric pressure with visible light irradiation. The amount of H2O2 generated by photocatalysis was determined by potassium permanganate titration. Fig. 5a shows the effect of different hydrothermal times on the photocatalytic performance. With the prolongation of the hydrothermal reaction time, the performance of the catalyst first increased and then decreased. Obviously, in the absence of CDs, no H2O2 is produced, indicating that CDs play a decisive role in the production of H2O2, but excessive CDs also lead to the deepening of the catalyst color and hinder the absorption of light. On the other hand, CDs have charge storage properties, and excess CDs can affect charge transport. In addition, the H2O2 produced by photocatalysts at different hydrothermal temperatures is shown in Fig. S15. The catalyst has achieved carbonization when the hydrothermal temperature is higher than 150 ℃· H2O2 production at different catalyst concentrations is shown in Fig. S16. The dependence plot of H2O2 generated by HP-2 under light irradiation with time is displayed in Fig. 5b. In the absence of light, no H2O2 is produced, thereby excluding the occurrence of dark reactions. After 4 h of illumination, the yield of H2O2 can reach 3896 μmol L−1, and the generation of H2O2 gradually stabilized after 6 h. The stability of the catalyst was tested by performing five photocatalytic cycling experiments on HP-2, as shown in Fig. 5c. The catalyst still maintained a high catalytic activity in the fifth cycling experiment. Moreover, the C1s spectra after reaction with HP-2 as catalyst shown in Fig. S17 further demonstrated the catalyst has good stability. Apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) can be used to evaluate the utilization of light energy by the catalyst. Fig. 5d reveals that the change trend of AQE at different wavelengths is consistent with the absorption spectrum, indicating that the generation of H2O2 is a light-driven reaction. To demonstrate the stability of this catalytic system, the H2O2 degradation experiments was tested under saturated nitrogen and oxygen atmosphere, and the experimental results are shown in Fig. S18. After saturated with nitrogen, there is no significant decrease in the concentration of H2O2 within 6 h of light exposure. Although nitrogen has been used to remove oxygen from water, HP-2 can also generate oxygen under light through water oxidation. The fact that the hydrogen peroxide concentration did not decrease significantly was due to the equilibrium between the production and degradation of H2O2. In addition, under saturated oxygen atmosphere, H2O2 concentration was constantly elevated under light since oxygen acts as a raw material to generate H2O2.

To explore the mechanism of H2O2 generation by photocatalysis, the H2O2 generation of HP-2 under different atmospheres were tested. Fig. 6a shows that the amount of H2O2 generated is higher in oxygen-saturated atmosphere (4480 μmol L−1) than in air (3896 μmol L−1), and almost no H2O2 is generated in nitrogen-saturated atmosphere, indicating that the increase of O2 is beneficial to the generation of H2O2. To further investigate the catalytic reaction mechanism, the H2O2 production when different active species were captured are shown in Fig. 6b. AgNO3, EDTA-2Na, TBA and BQ were used trapping agents of electron(e-), hole (h+), hydroxyl radical (·OH) and superoxide radical (·O2 -), respectively·H2O2 production is greatly reduced when the electrons are trapped, while the production is boosted when the holes are trapped. These results suggest that the presence of electrons favors H2O2 generation, demonstrating that H2O2 is generated by O2 reduction reaction. In addition, H2O2 production was not negatively affected when ·OH were trapped by TBA, which implies that ·OH were not involved in H2O2 generation, excluding the possibility of H2O oxidation reaction to produce H2O2. However, when ·O2 - was trapped by BQ, almost no H2O2 was produced, suggesting that ·O2 - are necessary intermediates for the production of H2O2. Therefore, the H2O2 generation is a process in which ·O2 - is essential intermediate and consume electrons. Figs. 6c and S19 are the DMPO spin-trapping EPR spectra of HP-2 with ·O2 - and ·OH, respectively. It is worth noting that HP-2 can produce obvious ·O2 - signal but no ·OH signal under light irradiation, which corresponding to the photocatalytic performance under different sacrificial agents mentioned above, again proving that H2O2 is generated by O2 reduction reaction. In addition, the i-t curve of H2O oxidation was tested by rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE). As shown in Fig. S20, the ring current did not change after adding irradiation, indicating that H2O oxidation is a 4e- process to generate O2. The determination of oxygen content under different conditions by GC-7900 gas chromatography in Fig. S21 further demonstrates the ability of water oxidation to produce oxygen. The RRDE measurements of HP-2 for ORR in Fig. S22 can be observed that the oxidation current was detected on the platinum ring electrode, which proves that HP-2 could reduce O2 to H2O2. The LSV curves of ORR and OER in Figs. 6d and S23 illustrate HP-2 possesses higher O2 reduction and O2 evolution activity, so HP-2 is considered to have higher H2O2 production activity. In order to determine the oxidation and reduction sites on the catalyst, the deposition sites of Ag and PbO2 particles were marked by HRTEM, where Ag particles were deposited by Ag ions reacting with photogenerated electrons, and PbO2 particles were deposited by oxidation of Pb ions by photogenerated holes. As shown in Figs. 6d and 5f, Ag particles were found near CDs, but no CDs were found near the PbO2 particles, which proves that the reduction site of the catalyst is near the CDs.

In order to further explore the catalytic reaction mechanism, in-situ TPV test was used to study the interfacial transfer of electrons during the catalytic reaction. The amount of consumed electrons (A) can be represented by the integral difference of the TPV curves under different atmospheres. Fig. 7a and b are the in-situ TPV patterns of HP-0 and HP-2 under different conditions, respectively. In this catalytic system, N2 does not participate in any reaction and can be used as a control. When the reaction occurs after adding H2O and O2, there is a certain change in the maximum amount of electrons generated by photoexcitation. As shown in Fig. 7a, when H2O and O2 were added to the reaction system, the amount of electrons generated by the photoexcitation of HP-0 have a little change, indicating that HP-0 had almost no ability to consume electrons and holes. In contrast, the in-situ TPV curves of HP-2 in Fig. 7b have obvious differences under different atmospheric conditions. Compared with N2 atmosphere, the addition of H2O and O2 to the reaction system has a greater effect on the amount of electrons. As can be seen from Fig. 7c, HP-2 has a higher photovoltage in the H2O-containing condition than saturated N2. The holes are consumed by the H2O oxidation reaction in the presence of H2O, leaving more electrons, where A1 represents the increased amount of electrons. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 7d, in the presence of O2, a large number of electrons are consumed by O2 reduction reaction, and A2 represents the amount of consumed electrons. The above shows that the HP-2 has the ability of H2O oxidation and O2 reduction, and the oxygen reduction activity is stronger.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration on the roles of H2O and O2
ce:section.ce:para: CWT analysis of HP-2 in different reaction conditions probed the roles of H2O and O2 for charge regulation. The FFT spectra of HP-2 under different reaction atmospheres are shown in Fig. S24a-c. Obviously, the curve intensity gradually decays with the increase of frequency without static and periodic frequency components, indicating that the TPV curves in different reaction atmospheres have non-static characteristics and can be analyzed by CWT. Fig. S24d-f show the two-dimension (2D) CWT spectra of HP-2 under different reaction conditions. Fig. S24g is an intensity-frequency curve at different time in a saturated O2 atmosphere. At t = 0.4 ms, the green curve has strong peaks in both low-frequency and high-frequency ranges, indicating that both low-frequency and high-frequency electron transport play an important role in the electron transfer process at this time. When the time gradually increased to t = 1.2 ms, the blue curve does not show peaks at the high-frequency, indicating that the high-frequency electron transport almost disappeared at this time. When t = 2.4 ms, the main peaks of the black curve are concentrated in the low-frequency range (<8 Hz), indicating that only low-frequency electron transport plays an important role at this time. The low-frequency electron transport exists in the whole electronic process, which is crucial for the progress of the catalytic reaction. Similarly, the intensity-frequency curves at different time under 0.5 vol% water/acetonitrile and N2 saturated conditions are shown in Fig. S24h and i.

In order to further study the effect of H2O and O2 on the charge behavior at low frequencies, the time difference between reaching the peak value under different conditions were compared in Figs. 8(a, b) and S25, and the average of the first three peaks is taken when multiple peaks exist. By comparing the time difference reaching the peak at low frequencies under N2 and aqueous conditions, a line graph as shown in Fig. 8c can be obtained. The time difference (tN2-tH2O) is greater than 0 at low frequencies, which was due to the polarity-induced effect of H2O accelerates the low-frequency electron transport. The peak delay time difference diagram at different frequencies under saturated N2 and O2 conditions is shown in Fig. 8d. At low frequencies, the time difference (tN2-tO2) is less than 0, oxygen has no polarity, so it cannot accelerate the transport of low-frequency electrons. In addition, the O2 reduction reaction occurs on the CDs, and the charge storage function of CDs restricts the transport of low-frequency electrons and prolongs the lifetime of low-frequency electrons. The EIS-Nyquist plots of HP-2 in 0.5 vol% water/acetonitrile and O2 saturated atmosphere are shown in Fig. 8e and f, which also prove that the presence of O2 makes low-frequency electron transport slower, while H2O accelerates the low-frequency electron transport. The roles of H2O and O2 in the catalytic process are shown in Fig. 8g. From the time difference (tN2-tH2O) at different frequencies in the table, H2O accelerates the transmission of low-frequency electrons, and H2O also acts as a reactant to generate O2 through the four-electron H2O oxidation process. The time difference (tN2-tO2) at different frequencies indicate that the participation of O2 slows down the transmission of low-frequency electrons and prolongs the lifetime of electrons. Besides, O2 as a reactant can generate H2O2 through two-electron O2 reduction reaction.

According to the above analysis, the schematic diagram of H2O2 production by photocatalysis in Fig. S26 can be obtained. Under visible light irradiation, the catalyst is excited to generate photogenerated electrons and holes. Photogenerated holes are can generate O2 through four-electron H2O oxidation process. The photogenerated electrons will be transferred to the surface of the CDs, which greatly facilitate the separation of photogenerated charges by the charge storage properties of the CDs. In addition, as reduction active centers, CDs can generate H2O2 through a two-electron O2 reduction process. The combination of four-electron and two-electron can avoid the poisoning of the catalyst by H2O2 as an intermediate product, improving the efficiency and stability of photocatalytic H2O2 generation.

In this work, a metal-free photocatalyst HP-2 containing in-situ formed CDs was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using hesperidin as raw material, and the photocatalytic yield of H2O2 could reach 4480 μmol L−1 without sacrificial agents under visible light. Notably, it is revealed that H2O and O2 can not only participate in the photocatalytic process as reactants, but also be used to regulate electron transport kinetic. The combination CWT with in-situ TPV tests demonstrated that the nonpolar nature of O2 can slow down the transport of low-frequency electrons, while the polarity-inducing effect of H2O can accelerate the transport of low-frequency electrons. This work provides a new idea for the study of catalytic systems involving H2O and O2. Furthermore, the first combined application of CWT and in-situ TPV testing deepens the exploration of the photocatalytic mechanism.

Yan Liu: Data curation, Visualization. Jiaxuan Wang: Investigation. Jie Wu: Formal analysis. Yajie Zhao: Investigation, Software. Hui Huang: Writing – original draft, Project administration. Yang Liu: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhenhui Kang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (2020YFA0406104, 2020YFA0406101), National MCF Energy Research and Development Program of China (2018YFE0306105), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51725204, 21771132, 51972216, 52041202‬), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041), Key-Area Research and Development Program of Guangdong Province (2019B010933001), Key Research and Development Program of Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region (2022BEG02006), Ningxia Autonomous Region Flexible Introduction of Science and Technology Innovation Team (2021RXTDLX08), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the 111 Project, and Suzhou Key Laboratory of Functional Nano & Soft Materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.apcatb.2022.121944.
